skill.name: emerging-topic-scout
skill.description: A real-time monitoring system for identifying research hotspots in biological and medical sciences before they are defined by mainstream journals.
skill.summary: Monitors preprint servers to detect emerging research topics using trend analysis algorithms.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 212 static findings were evaluated and dismissed as false positives. The skill is a legitimate academic research monitoring tool that fetches preprints from arXiv, bioRxiv, and medRxiv via RSS feeds and APIs. Hardcoded URLs point exclusively to academic preprint servers. Backtick patterns detected in Markdown documentation are code formatting, not Ruby shell execution. DOIs identified as 'weak cryptographic algorithm' are persistent identifiers, not cryptographic hashes. No credential access, data exfiltration, obfuscation, or prompt injection was found. The skill has legitimate network and filesystem operations for its intended purpose. Risk level set to LOW due to the presence of network and filesystem capabilities that users should be aware of.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs to Academic Preprint Servers
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged hardcoded URLs for arXiv, bioRxiv, and medRxiv RSS feeds and APIs. These URLs are legitimate academic endpoints required for the skill's core function of fetching preprint metadata. No evidence of malicious endpoints or data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Clear context: URLs point to public academic preprint APIs (arxiv.org, biorxiv.org, medrxiv.org) and documentation/reference sites. No credential parameters or suspicious endpoints present.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Formatting Misidentified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged backtick characters in SKILL.md and references/README.md as Ruby/shell backtick execution. These are Markdown code formatting characters used to document CLI usage examples, not executable commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All instances are within Markdown code fences showing example python script invocations. Backticks are Markdown syntax, not command execution. Documentation-only files.

low_findings.title: DOI Identifiers Misidentified as Cryptographic Algorithms
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged DOI patterns (10.1101/..., 10.xxxx/...) as weak cryptographic algorithms. DOIs are Digital Object Identifiers used to identify academic papers, not cryptographic implementations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: DOI regex pattern 10.\d{4,}/[^\s<>"']+ is used to extract paper identifiers from RSS feed entries. No cryptographic algorithms, keys, or hashing functions exist in the codebase.

low_findings.title: Print Statements Misidentified as System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged print() calls and diagnostic output as system reconnaissance. These are status messages showing scan progress, paper counts, and results. No system information gathering beyond the skill's operational scope.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Print statements output progress messages like 'Analyzing N papers...' and 'Extracted N keywords'. No filesystem listing, environment variable dumps, or network scanning calls.

low_findings.title: DOI String Misidentified as Hardcoded IP Address
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged DOI reference '10.1101/2022.04.01.486720' in references/README.md as a hardcoded IP address. This is an academic paper identifier, not a network IP.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged string is a DOI used to reference an academic paper citation. The dot-separated numbers happen to resemble an IP address format but are a date-based identifier.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Write Operations for History and Output
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged file write operations in main.py. These are legitimate: writing trend history to data/history.json and saving user-requested output via --save flag. No unauthorized file access or tampering.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 254 writes trend history to a dedicated data directory. Line 689 writes formatted output only when user specifies --save flag. Paths are constrained to skill directory or user-specified locations.

content.user_title: Track emerging biomedical trends from preprint servers
content.value_statement: Researchers and analysts need to spot emerging topics before they appear in major journals. This skill automates the process by scanning arXiv, bioRxiv, and medRxiv preprints using trend analysis algorithms that detect keyword clusters, momentum shifts, and cross-platform mentions.
actual_capabilities: Fetches recent preprints from arXiv q-bio, bioRxiv, and medRxiv using RSS feeds and APIs
Extracts keywords and compound biomedical terms from paper titles and abstracts
Calculates trending scores using frequency, momentum, and novelty components
Clusters related keywords into emerging topics with velocity classification
Outputs results in JSON, Markdown, or CSV format with paper citations
Saves historical scan data for trend comparison and duplicate detection
limitations: bioRxiv and medRxiv RSS feeds are currently blocked by Cloudflare, reducing available data sources
Trend analysis is based on keyword frequency, not deep semantic understanding of research content
Requires network access to external preprint servers and APIs to function
Topic clustering uses simple co-occurrence rather than advanced NLP topic modeling

use_cases.title: Weekly literature monitoring for research groups
use_cases.description: A lab principal runs a weekly scan of arXiv q-bio to identify new research directions in their field before they become mainstream, helping prioritize reading and collaboration.
use_cases.target_user: Academic research group leaders and principal investigators

use_cases.title: Grant proposal trend analysis
use_cases.description: A grant writer scans preprint servers for emerging topics to identify high-impact research areas for funding proposals, using the trending score to justify novelty and timeliness.
use_cases.target_user: Grant writers and research development professionals

use_cases.title: Competitive intelligence for biomedical R&D
use_cases.description: An industry analyst monitors preprint activity to detect early signals of competitor research directions or breakthrough technologies in the biomedical space.

prompt_templates.title: Quick daily scan with arXiv
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a quick scan of emerging biomedical research topics using arXiv q-bio as the source. Look back 1 day and output results in markdown format. Show me the top topics with their trending scores.
prompt_templates.scenario: Brief daily check for new research topics

prompt_templates.title: Deep weekly analysis with specific keywords
prompt_templates.prompt: Scan arXiv q-bio for the past 14 days using keywords related to CRISPR, gene editing, and machine learning. Set minimum trending score to 0.7 and return up to 20 topics. Output as JSON. Save the results to a weekly report file.
prompt_templates.scenario: Focused deep analysis of specific research areas

prompt_templates.title: Multi-source comparison scan
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare emerging topics across arXiv, bioRxiv, and medRxiv over the past 7 days. Note that bioRxiv and medRxiv may not return results due to Cloudflare protection. Output a markdown report highlighting topics that appear across multiple sources.

prompt_templates.title: Trend velocity analysis with history
prompt_templates.prompt: Scan arXiv q-bio for the last 30 days and classify topics by growth velocity: explosive, rapid, moderate, or slow. Use the history data to identify which topics have been consistently trending versus newly emergent. Output as markdown with velocity indicators.
prompt_templates.scenario: Long-term trend monitoring with velocity classification

output_examples.input: Scan arXiv q-bio for the past 7 days and show me the top emerging topics in markdown format.
Scan arXiv q-bio for the past 30 days and output the results as JSON, saving to a file named weekly_report.json.

best_practices: Start with a 1-day scan using arXiv only to verify the skill functions correctly before running longer or multi-source scans.
Use the --min-score flag to filter low-relevance topics, starting at 0.6 for broad discovery and increasing to 0.8 for focused results.
Save scan history by keeping the data/history.json file between runs, which enables trend velocity calculation and novelty detection.
anti_patterns: Do not rely on bioRxiv or medRxiv as the sole data source for critical decisions, as their RSS feeds are currently blocked by Cloudflare.
Do not treat the trending score as a definitive measure of research quality or impact. It is a keyword frequency metric and should be validated manually.
Do not run scans without specifying --sources, as the default includes blocked sources and may return empty results.

faq.question: Which preprint servers does this skill support?
faq.answer: The skill supports arXiv q-bio (Quantitative Biology), bioRxiv (biology), and medRxiv (medicine). arXiv is the recommended source. bioRxiv and medRxiv are currently blocked by Cloudflare.

faq.question: Why are bioRxiv and medRxiv not working?
faq.answer: These servers use Cloudflare JavaScript Challenge protection which blocks programmatic RSS access. Use arXiv as an alternative source for reliable data fetching.

faq.question: What output formats are available?
faq.answer: Three output formats are supported: markdown for readable reports, JSON for programmatic processing, and CSV for spreadsheet analysis. Use the --output flag to choose.

faq.question: How is the trending score calculated?
faq.answer: The score combines three components: frequency (30 percent), momentum of recent mentions (40 percent), and novelty based on historical data (30 percent). Compound terms receive a small boost.

faq.question: Does this skill require internet access?
faq.answer: Yes, the skill requires network access to reach arXiv, bioRxiv, and medRxiv servers. It cannot function offline. Results are saved locally when using the --save flag.

faq.question: Can I track specific research keywords over time?
faq.answer: Yes, use the --keywords flag to specify comma-separated terms. The skill also auto-detects keywords from fetched papers. Historical data is stored for trend comparison across scans.
